comments.comment_text:
My partner and I have two SEND children. One went to a specialist school run by the state as a result of his EHCP. He was non-verbal when he moved from state school. He is now participating in specialist further education and completing work experience. The other is in a state school and can't get an EHCP because the county council refuse to assess him (he has been assessed by CAMHS). Getting him to school each day is a constant fight and is damaging to his and our wellbeing. 

The lack of money for support is ruining lives and, with respect, is a direct consequence of globalisation, the move to service industries from production, and privatisation of public services. Money that used to stay in (and be taxed in) the UK is now going to international companies and shareholders that don't contribute back into the Treasury at anywhere near the rate that UK workers and UK based companies did (and still do). 

We've created an economy that is essentially asset stripping our citizens and our public sector (a form of managed decline). No amount of productivity gains is going to resolve this issue, and no amount of balancing the books is going to stop it from getting worse. comments.comment_text:
As a parent of a daughter with an EHCP, I am exhausted by having to fight every single year for support that my child is legally entitled to receive. Our children are not statistics. They are not budget lines. They are human beings with hopes, vulnerabilities, and enormous potential — and they deserve to be treated as such.
Each year we attend an annual review, and instead of feeling supported, we feel as though we are entering a battle. Families are forced to justify their child’s needs over and over again, as if those needs somehow disappear from one year to the next. The council has an eight-week deadline to respond following a review. We are still waiting for a decision from late 2024. The silence is not just frustrating — it is destabilising for families trying to plan their children’s futures.
The transition to secondary school under these circumstances feels like a failure waiting to happen. The current four-to-five-year waiting list for diagnosis is simply unacceptable. Early intervention matters. My daughter was diagnosed in 2020, just ten days before lockdown — and even with that diagnosis, we are still fighting for appropriate provision.
She has gone from receiving full-time 1:1 support to only half days. Yes, she is currently in mainstream primary, but those who truly know her understand that a mainstream secondary setting — particularly given her limited awareness of danger and safety — would present overwhelming challenges without the right support in place.
What hurts most is that parents should not have to become legal experts and campaigners just to secure what the law already says our children deserve. We should be able to trust the system designed to protect and support them.
I have fought for my daughter every year, and I will continue to fight. But it should not take constant battles for children with additional needs to be seen, heard, and properly supported.

comments.comment_text:
A couple things with disability benefits. Firstly, each pound spent on something like PIP gives back to the economy more than is spent - in terms of government systems the cost ratio is something that would be ideal to have for any project, but it gets attacked a lot because of the idea that the cost going up means more people not working and that we’re somehow losing money. 

Secondly, the assessments for these benefits are so poorly done and systemically cruel that over the last 10+ years the DWP has effectively created a hostile relationship between it and any benefit claimant, and therefore any disabled person.
For context, my partner needs a wheelchair, and has a number of conditions that means she’ll never be able to work. I have seen a one hour assessment take 4 hours to complete, I’ve seen reports deny existence of evidence of diagnosed conditions or impacts that are clearly documented. I’ve seen a claim to renew an existing PIP award get rejected outright, only to go to tribunal and have the DWP not even bother to send a representative. Everyone I’ve talked to or heard from about PIP and the DWP assessment process has shared that it’s a nightmare, and the animosity for the DWP is clear. This is a process that in most cases needs to be repeated every 3 years, regardless of how permanent a person’s conditions are, and where a high administrative burden is intentionally placed on claimants to try and exhaust them into giving up. I wouldn’t feel confident saying that if I hadn’t seen the case where the DWP didn’t send a representative to argue their case in tribunal, and been told by the charity we contacted for our representation that as many as 1/3 cases they saw the DWP wouldn’t send a rep. The number of decisions overturned at tribunal is really high - it’s wasting a lot of time and money trying to deny people what they’re legally entitled to. Under these conditions the government and DWP has become not a source of support to be thankful for, but an adversary that has to be fought to get the support you need. Under these conditions I don’t think any change to the system will be viewed with intense scrutiny and suspicion. It is seriously contributing to existing mental illnesses to have to fight these systems and presume the person assessing you is looking for reasons to claim you aren’t disabled

comments.comment_text:
Rubio told many in Europe what they wanted to hear. But Rory is correct - it's bullshit and the US will dump anyone it wants for it's own benefit, it's just clearer now.
The UK needs to prepare for conflict, because war is looming, because the Russian leaders haven't got a choice, they keep pushing or implode. If we aren't prepared to fight we will have to fight. If we are strong we may be able to deter direct conflict and cause the Russian leaders to disintegrate.  Hopefully this time well step in and help the Russian people so that they don't turn to yet another set of extreme dictators.

comments.comment_text: I'm American and I can admit that I'm not fully plugged into the intricate details of UK politics, but listening to this makes me wonder if Starmer even knows what the people in power here think, say, and want to do. Is he in complete denial about what the US is like right now. Rory is 100% correct. Carney is a strong leader, I just don't get why he would insult him while placating our dictator in charge.
